title: LiftRPG Booklet Schema v1.3
description: Renderer-first schema. The renderer owns the artifact. The LLM fills named slots. Layout never touches the LLM. Every field marked RENDERER means the renderer consumes it structurally. Every field marked LLM means the LLM is responsible for its content quality. Fields marked GENERATOR are set at generation time from LiftOScript or system logic, not by the LLM. v1.1: added isDeload, reEntryRule. v1.2: replaced passwordCharacter with weeklyComponent system, added isBossWeek + bossEncounter, added oracle consequence type, binaryChoice. v1.3: removed anomalyPrompt (notes box reframe — no form field on session cards; Invisible Until Engaged principle applied; see brief v1.7 Section 05A). Updated binaryChoice rendering to map-marker circles. Updated bossEncounter for narrative-first design with partial convergence.

meta.description: Block-level constants. Set once. Consumed everywhere.

schemaVersion.description: GENERATOR. Always '1.3.0'. Renderer uses this to validate compatibility.

blockTitle.description: LLM. The full title of this block's story. Rendered in Playfair Display Italic on cover and spine. Example: 'The Cartographer of Unmapped Rooms'.

blockSubtitle.description: LLM. One line. Appears beneath title on cover in Share Tech Mono. Should feel like an official designation, not a tagline.

worldContract.description: LLM. One sentence. The fundamental nature of this world and what it asks of the person inside it. This is the north star for every design and story decision in the block. Must be written before any other LLM field is generated.

narrativeVoice.description: LLM. The epistemological stance of the narration. Not a stylistic preference — a structural decision with mechanical implications for the unlock.

person.description: LLM. Grammatical person for session card prompts.

tense.description: LLM. Tense for session card prompts.

narratorStance.description: LLM. One sentence. Who is speaking, to whom, and under what conditions. Example: 'The narrator is writing field notes in real time, aware they may not be retrieved.' This sentence is included verbatim in the LLM generation prompt for all session content.

voiceRationale.description: LLM. Why this specific voice serves this specific world contract. Must connect directly to worldContract — not 'first person feels intimate' but 'first person past tense positions the narrator as someone who survived to write this, which creates dramatic irony the world contract requires.' If this rationale does not reference the world contract, the voice is wrong.

literaryRegister.description: LLM. The tonal and formal world of this block's prose.

name.description: LLM. Internal reference name only. Never printed in the booklet. Used in generation prompts.

behaviorDescription.description: LLM. What this register does — as properties and behaviors, never source names. 'Prose that accumulates weight through repetition, where the landscape thinks back' not 'Steinbeck-style.' Three to five sentences.

forbiddenMoves.description: LLM. What this register must never do. At least three specific prohibitions. These are included in every generation prompt for session content and found documents.

typographicBehavior.description: LLM. How typography participates in this register — not decorates it. How it should behave in week 1, how it might develop, what fracture (if any) is permitted and when. Must reference the anti-formulaic rule: changes feel discovered, not scheduled.

weeklyComponentType.description: LLM. The single component type this block uses across all non-boss weeks. Defined once here, consistent everywhere. Must feel native to the world contract — a surveyor's block uses coordinates or room designations; an interrogation block might use case numbers or dates; a correspondence block might use postal codes or initials. Examples: 'letter', 'coordinate-fragment', 'date-fragment', 'room-designation', 'case-number'. The boss week's convergence mechanism is designed around these components.

structuralShape.description: LLM. The story's structural logic — decided at generation time, before content generation, as part of the property extraction stage. Must differ meaningfully from the previous block's structural shape. These decisions affect every generation prompt downstream.

resolution.description: LLM. Does the story reach a conclusion, refuse to, or reach one that raises more than it closes?

temporalOrder.description: LLM. Does the story move chronologically, reveal backward from an endpoint, expand from a central event, or deliberately resist ordering?

narratorReliability.description: LLM. Is the narrator trustworthy? 'Unknown' means the reader cannot determine this. 'Multiple' means different voices have different reliability levels.

promptFragmentRelationship.description: LLM. The relationship between session card prompts and back-of-book fragments. Parallel: both tell the same story from different angles. Fragments-deepen: prompts are true; fragments add complexity. Fragments-contradict: fragments reveal the prompts were wrong. Prompts-are-the-lie: the narrator's own voice is the unreliable layer. Fragments-are-the-lie: the found documents are fabricated; the prompts are the only true thing.

shapeRationale.description: LLM. Why this structural shape serves this world contract. Must reference the worldContract and the extracted properties. If a previous block's structuralShape is known, must confirm the shape differs and explain why.

passwordPlaintext.description: GENERATOR. The complete password — a real word or phrase meaningful within the block's world, stripped to uppercase alphanumeric. Produced by the boss week's convergence mechanism applied to all prior weekly components. Used to AES-encrypt the endings. Never rendered anywhere in the booklet. Validated against the boss week's convergenceProof at generation time. Generation fails if proof doesn't produce this string.

passwordEncryptedEnding.description: GENERATOR. AES-256-GCM encrypted blob of the endings array, keyed on passwordPlaintext. This string is what gets delivered to liftrpg.co — the server never sees the key. Base64url encoded.

liftoScript.description: GENERATOR. The raw LiftOScript programme pasted by the user. Stored verbatim for audit and reproducibility. The renderer does not parse this — it is parsed upstream and its output drives weekCount, sessions per week, and exercise data.

weekCount.description: GENERATOR. Derived from LiftOScript. Drives weeks array length validation.

totalSessions.description: GENERATOR. Total session count across all weeks. Used in page budget calculation.

weeks.description: One object per week. Length must equal meta.weekCount.

title.description: LLM. Rendered in Playfair Display Italic as the week heading. Should feel like a chapter title in the world — not a workout label. Example: 'Survey Sector 4 · The Rooms That Close'.

epigraph.description: LLM. Optional but strongly encouraged. A single line — a fragment of found text, a partial coordinate, a date with no event attached — rendered in Share Tech Mono beneath the week title. Carries atmosphere. May contain embedded narrative information. Not a quote from a named source.

attribution.description: LLM. The apparent source — an internal document designation, a name, a form number. Never a real author. Example: 'MSA Internal Memo 44-C' or 'Reyes, undated'.

isBossWeek.description: GENERATOR. True for the final week only. When true: fieldOps is replaced by bossEncounter; the right page type changes entirely; the LLM generation prompt receives the full prior weeklyComponent list and must produce a boss that genuinely requires them all. Exactly one week per block has isBossWeek: true.

weeklyComponent.description: LLM + GENERATOR. The component this week contributes toward the boss convergence. Type is block-specific — defined once in meta and consistent across all weeks. On the boss week, this field is null (the boss produces the complete password, not another component).

type.description: LLM. The kind of component this block uses. Defined at block level and consistent across all weeks. Examples: 'letter', 'coordinate', 'date-fragment', 'number', 'symbol'. Must feel native to the world contract — not a mechanical overlay.

value.description: GENERATOR. The actual component value. Null on the boss week. Never rendered in the booklet in plaintext. Used by the validator to confirm the boss convergenceProof produces meta.passwordPlaintext.

extractionInstruction.description: LLM. The in-world instruction for extracting and recording this week's component. Rendered in Share Tech Mono beneath the cipher workspace. Fiction-first — should feel like a field procedure. Must be completable in under 90 seconds in a gym context.

sessions.description: GENERATOR length, LLM content. Length driven by LiftOScript. Content (prompts, refs) by LLM.

label.description: GENERATOR. Rendered in Share Tech Mono in the card header. Example: 'Session 2 · Workout B'. Derived from LiftOScript.

exercises.description: GENERATOR. Populated entirely from LiftOScript. LLM does not touch exercise data.

name.description: GENERATOR. Exercise name as written in LiftOScript.

sets.description: GENERATOR. Number of sets — drives how many rep-box rows are rendered.

repsPerSet.description: GENERATOR. Integer for fixed reps (renders N rep boxes per row). String for schemes like 'AMRAP' or '5/3/1' (rendered as label, single wide field). Range strings like '8-12' render the scheme as a label with a single log field.

weightField.description: GENERATOR. True renders a weight input field after the rep boxes. False for bodyweight movements.

notes.description: GENERATOR. Optional. Tempo, rest period, or cue from LiftOScript. Rendered in Share Tech Mono beneath the exercise row at reduced size.

storyPrompt.description: LLM. Required. 2–4 sentences. Second person or first person as established by the block's narrativeVoice. Present tense. Each prompt must advance the story, establish atmosphere, or recontextualise something. Never summarise. The prompt should create a thread the reader wants to follow. The notes box below the exercise table is the space for personal reflection; the rules spread frames it in world voice as the place surveyors note anomalies they haven't filed officially. No separate anomaly field exists — the story prompt does the atmospheric work.

fragmentRef.description: LLM. Optional. Rendered as '→ F.14' in Share Tech Mono at the bottom of the story prompt. Points to a fragment in the fragments array. Only present when the session prompt creates a thread that a specific fragment resolves or deepens — not as regular decoration.

binaryChoice.description: LLM. Optional. Present on exactly one session card per block, at the midpoint week. Never on the boss week. Presents two genuine narrative continuations. Rendered as two short prose options separated by a thin rule, each preceded by an empty map-marker circle (○ not □) — no 'choose one' label, no A/B designation. The fork is discovered in the story, not presented as a form. Lifter fills one circle. No mechanical consequence. Subsequent session prompts subtly acknowledge which path was taken. Boss week document may contain a detail that resonates with one path only, unremarked.

choiceLabel.description: LLM. The in-world framing of the decision, rendered above the two options. Should feel like a moment of genuine consequence, not a game mechanic. Example: 'Reyes left a door open. Survey Conduct Protocol requires you to report it. You have not reported it yet.'

promptA.description: LLM. One sentence. The first continuation. Rendered with a circle-A marker.

promptB.description: LLM. One sentence. The second continuation. Rendered with a circle-B marker.

bossEncounter.description: LLM. Required when isBossWeek is true. The boss page is the most important found document in the booklet. It is designed as narrative climax first — worth reading on its own terms regardless of component collection. The convergence mechanism lives INSIDE the found document as a natural feature of its content, not as a separate puzzle box. Three collection states must be designed explicitly: (0-1 components) narrative climax only — complete as an experience, convergence mechanism invisible without components; (2-3 components) partial convergence — visible progress, a real experience not a failure state; (4-5 components) full convergence — password resolves in a single moment. The convergenceProof must demonstrate all three states are intentional. The boss form differs completely every block — derived from the world contract, never predicted from the previous block's boss.

title.description: LLM. The in-world title of the boss page. Should feel like a final report designation, a terminal form, or a document that was never meant to be completed. Not 'Final Puzzle'.

narrative.description: LLM. 2–4 sentences. The story moment that frames the boss encounter. Written in the block's narrative voice. This is the climax of the story layer — it should earn the weight the block has built toward.

mechanismDescription.description: LLM. The rendered instructions for the boss puzzle — in-world, fiction-first. What the user actually does with their collected components. Must be completable with the booklet and a pencil. Must require all prior components. Must not feel like a puzzle instruction — it should feel like a procedure, a revelation, a final step in a protocol.

componentInputs.description: GENERATOR. The ordered list of prior weeks' component values, passed to the LLM so the boss mechanism can be designed around them. Validator uses this to confirm convergenceProof.

convergenceProof.description: VALIDATOR. A step-by-step walkthrough of the full convergence (all 5 components) that must produce passwordPlaintext exactly. Generator hard-fails if this proof does not resolve correctly. Also must document: (a) what a 0-component user experiences when reading the boss document — what they see, what the narrative resolution is for them; (b) what a 2-3 component user can derive — what partial progress looks like and why it feels like a real experience not a locked door.

passwordRevealInstruction.description: LLM. The final in-world instruction — what to do with the word that has just appeared. Should acknowledge the weight of the moment without explaining it. Then: 'Enter it at liftrpg.co.' Rendered at the bottom of the boss page in Share Tech Mono.

binaryChoiceAcknowledgement.description: LLM. Optional. If the block has a binaryChoice, the boss page may acknowledge which path was taken. Printed as two variants with a conditional marker.

properties.description: RENDERER structure, LLM content. The right page of a standard week spread.

mapState.description: GENERATOR/RENDERER. The survey grid as it exists at the end of this week.

floorLabel.description: LLM. Rendered as map header. Example: 'Floor 3 · Sub-Basement Survey'. Should feel like an official designation with something slightly wrong about it.

tiles.description: GENERATOR. Each tile in row-major order.

type.description: RENDERER. Each type maps to a distinct fill pattern (not colour) for grayscale safety: empty=outline only, cleared=diagonal crosshatch, locked=dot fill, anomaly=dense crosshatch+border, current=solid fill, inaccessible=solid fill no border.

label.description: LLM. Optional. Room designation rendered at small size inside the tile. Example: 'B-7' or '✓' or a single redaction block.

annotation.description: LLM. Optional. Rendered as a marginal note adjacent to the tile — the surveyor's handwritten-style note. Max 8 words.

currentPosition.description: GENERATOR. Drives the 'current' tile type — explicit here for validation.

mapNote.description: LLM. Optional. A single line rendered below the map in Share Tech Mono. Should feel like a field update or an anomaly report. The kind of note that is factual and alarming.

cipher.description: LLM content, RENDERER structure. The puzzle that yields this week's password character.

type.description: LLM. The mechanical type of this cipher. Difficulty should escalate across weeks — not uniformly, but with variance. Week 1 should be solvable in under 60 seconds. By week 5 an LLM assist is expected and acceptable.

title.description: LLM. Rendered as the cipher section header. Should feel like an official designation within the world — a form number, a procedure name — not 'Puzzle'.

body.description: LLM. The renderable cipher content. Structure varies by type — renderer uses 'type' field to select the correct rendering template.

displayText.description: LLM. The primary cipher text, grid, or encoded string. What the reader sees and works with.

key.description: LLM. Optional key material rendered alongside — a partial alphabet, a number sequence, a grid legend. Omit when the key is embedded in found documents or fragments (cross-reference ciphers).

workSpace.description: RENDERER. Blank working area rendered below the cipher. Dimensioned based on expected solve complexity.

style.description: RENDERER. Dot grid for open-ended work. Ruled for linear decoding. Blank for spatial puzzles. Box grid for letter-by-letter substitution.

noticeabilityDesign.description: LLM. REQUIRED. How an unwarned reader — someone who has not read the rules page — would discover that a puzzle mechanic exists here at all. Noticeability is the primary design requirement. If the answer is 'they wouldn't', this cipher has failed before it has been read. Describe the specific visual or narrative cue that makes the cipher's existence legible without prior briefing.

extractionInstruction.description: LLM. The in-world instruction for extracting and recording this week's component from the solved cipher. Rendered in Share Tech Mono beneath the workspace. Fiction-first — should feel like a field procedure. Must be completable in under 90 seconds in a gym context. Must reference the component type defined in meta. Example: 'Log the 7th character of the decoded sequence on your Survey Record, p.02.'

characterDerivationProof.description: LLM + GENERATOR. Plain English walkthrough of exactly how solving this cipher yields the correct weekly component value. Validator only — never rendered. Must confirm that solving the published puzzle produces exactly weeks[n].weeklyComponent.value. Generation hard-fails if this proof does not check out.

oracleTable.description: LLM. Roll 2d6 during rest. 11 results. Most point to fragments; up to 3 per week may be consequence entries that instruct the reader to update the map or a document. The distinction is in the text — consequence entries render identically to fragment entries in layout.

title.description: LLM. Section header. Should feel like an official form designation. Example: 'Survey Incident Classification Table · MSA Form 7-R'.

instruction.description: LLM. One line. The in-world instruction for using the table. Rendered in Share Tech Mono. Should feel like a procedure, not a game rule.

entries.description: LLM. Exactly 11 entries covering 2d6 results (2–12). Maximum 3 entries may be type 'consequence'. The rest are type 'fragment'.

text.description: LLM. The incident classification or result text. Should feel like an entry in an official taxonomy — dry, precise, unsettling. One line. For consequence entries, this is the consequence instruction itself — completable with a pencil in a 90-second rest window. Example: 'Sector 3-B is now inaccessible. Mark it on your survey grid.'

type.description: LLM. 'fragment' directs the reader to a fragment. 'consequence' instructs an action — updating the map, annotating a document. No special styling differentiates them in rendering — the distinction lives only in the text.

fragmentRef.description: LLM. Required when type is 'fragment'. Which fragment this result directs the reader to.

overflow.description: GENERATOR. True when sessions.length > 3. Triggers Part 2 spread rendering. The overflow right page becomes a found document — see overflowDocument.

isDeload.description: GENERATOR. True when the LiftOScript programme specifies progress:none for all days in this week, indicating a deload. Never rendered explicitly in the booklet — the word 'deload' never appears. Passed to the LLM generation prompt as a tonal instruction: session card prompts should reflect reduced intensity, the narrative breathes rather than escalates, field operations may be lighter in mechanical content. The week is felt differently, not labelled differently.

overflowDocument.description: LLM. Required when overflow is true. The found document that occupies the right page of the Part 2 overflow spread. Drawn from the foundDocumentTypes taxonomy.

interlude.description: LLM. Optional. A typographic interlude page. Never generated by default — must be explicitly placed with a reason. 'It felt like a good moment' is not a reason.

reason.description: LLM. REQUIRED. Why this interlude exists at this specific moment. Must reference the narrative arc, the register's typographic behavior, or a specific structural function. Validator fails if reason does not reference worldContract or literaryRegister.typographicBehavior.

content.description: LLM. The text of the interlude. A single word, a short fragment, a number, a date, a coordinate, a partial sentence. Should feel torn from something larger.

scale.description: RENDERER. display = large headline scale. giant = fills most of the page. bleed = type runs off one or more edges intentionally.

inkCoverage.description: RENDERER. light = text only, white ground. dark = reversed, ink-heavy. split = used sparingly.

spreadAware.description: RENDERER. True = interlude occupies both pages of a spread.

payload.description: LLM. Whether the interlude carries mechanical information.

fragments.description: Back-of-book found documents. Non-sequential, mixed types. The reader navigates here via fragmentRef pointers scattered across session cards and oracle tables. Not a numbered list — each is a distinct document artifact.

title.description: LLM. Same as meta.blockTitle. Rendered in Playfair Display Italic at display scale.

designation.description: LLM. The official-sounding designation of this document within the world. Rendered in Share Tech Mono. Example: 'MSA Survey Log · Block 01 · Restricted Distribution'.

tagline.description: LLM. One line. Should feel like an official warning or a procedural note — not marketing copy. Example: 'Do not remove from assigned personnel. Return to Survey Authority upon block completion.'

colophonLines.description: GENERATOR + LLM. Small print at cover bottom: generated-at date, schema version, LiftOScript hash, liftrpg.co URL. Plus one LLM line that belongs to the world.

rulesSpread.description: The How to Play spread. Left page: instructions. Right page: password record log.

title.description: LLM. The in-world title for the rules page. Should feel like an official briefing document title, not 'How to Play'. Example: 'Personnel Orientation · Survey Conduct & Incident Protocol'.

reEntryRule.description: GENERATOR + LLM. Programme-specific re-entry logic derived from the LiftOScript progression type at generation time. Rendered as a distinct half-page section on the rules spread. Also referenced by a pointer on each week header.

progressionType.description: GENERATOR. The primary progression type detected in the LiftOScript. Drives which re-entry rule template is selected.

ruleText.description: LLM. The re-entry rule written in the voice of the world. Non-moralising. Specific to the progression type. Should read as a field procedure or institutional protocol — not a penalty, not a consolation. Example for lp(): 'If a survey week was skipped, repeat the current week's prescribed loads before advancing. The building does not accelerate on your behalf.' Must be actionable in under 30 seconds of reading.

sections.description: LLM. 4–6 sections covering: using the log, following fragment references, the cipher system, the oracle table, the password record, and liftrpg.co. Written in the voice of the world — an official briefing, not a game manual.

rightPage.description: RENDERER. The password record page. Renderer generates this from week count — one slot per week, labelled by week number, with a single character entry box. Plus the liftrpg.co URL and unlock instruction.

title.description: LLM. In-world title for the password record. Example: 'Survey Authentication Log · Record of Derived Designations'.

instruction.description: LLM. One paragraph. In-world instruction for recording and using the password. Should feel like a security procedure, not a game instruction.

endings.description: LLM. The final page content delivered on successful password entry at liftrpg.co. This array is AES-encrypted and stored in meta.passwordEncryptedEnding. The renderer never sees this in plaintext after generation. Design the ending for the person who earned it — the committed 15%, not the median user.

variant.description: LLM. What determines which ending is shown — a property of the password itself, a choice made at liftrpg.co, or the single canonical ending if minItems=1.

documentType.description: LLM. The found document type of the ending. The ending is not prose — it is the final found document. The one that explains, or refuses to explain, everything.

body.description: LLM. The full text of the ending document. No length constraint — this must be commensurate with the full block of effort. Not a resolution. A revelation. Or the precise shape of the absence of one.

finalLine.description: LLM. The last line. Designed as a discrete unit. The thing that stays.

designSpec.description: LLM. How the ending page is designed — what it looks like, what it feels like to arrive at it. Not a layout spec — a designed experience description. The reveal moment is a product decision, not a content decision.

foundDocument.description: A found document. Could be a back-of-book fragment, an overflow right page, or the ending. Each must pass the Found Document Authenticity tests from the quality rubric.

id.description: GENERATOR. Fragment identifier as rendered in the booklet.

documentType.description: LLM. From the Found Document Taxonomy. 'anomaly' is for the one item that is none of the others and should not exist.

inWorldAuthor.description: LLM. Who wrote this document within the world. Must be specific — not 'unknown' unless the unknownness is itself a designed choice with narrative function.

inWorldRecipient.description: LLM. Who this document was written for within the world.

inWorldPurpose.description: LLM. Why this document exists within the world — independent of its function in the story. The document should be plausible as an artifact of ordinary institutional or personal life, not as a story delivery mechanism.

content.description: LLM. The full text of the document, including any headers, form fields, redactions [██████], marginal annotations {in braces}, or procedural formatting appropriate to its type.

designSpec.description: RENDERER. How this document is visually distinguished from others — its typography, paper tone, and any unique layout features that match the institution that produced it.

paperTone.description: RENDERER. Maps to CSS background variants. warm=#e8dfc8, neutral=#ece6d8, cold=#e8ecf0, aged=#e0d8c8.

primaryTypeface.description: RENDERER. mono = Share Tech Mono throughout (bureaucratic). serif = Libre Baskerville (personal correspondence). mixed = mono headers, serif body (inspection reports, formal memos).

headerStyle.description: RENDERER. form = structured fields. letterhead = address block. handwritten = simulated by italic mono. none = document begins without header.

hasRedactions.description: RENDERER. True renders [██████] spans as solid black fills.

hasAnnotations.description: RENDERER. True renders {bracketed text} as marginal notes in a contrasting typeface at reduced size.

authenticityChecks.description: LLM. Validator only — never rendered. Forces LLM to verify each document against the quality rubric's Found Document Authenticity tests before generation is accepted.

hasIrrelevantDetail.description: LLM. True if the document contains at least one piece of information that is irrelevant to the plot — the noise that real documents contain. If false, generation fails.

couldExistInDifferentStory.description: LLM. True means the document is too generic — it could belong to any story of this type. False is required: the best found documents are specific enough that they could only belong to this world. If true, generation fails.

redactionDoesNarrativeWork.description: LLM. Null if no redactions present. True if redactions reveal through what they hide. False means the redactions are decorative — generation fails if hasRedactions is true and this is false.
